ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Mini Oreo sandwich cookie; 'cookie (food product)' used as the closest broader class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKN covers the biscuits/cookies/crackers category; no specific Oreo mini cookie code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific packaged cookie product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single-serve mini pack of Mini Oreo cookies weighs approximately 29 g (about 9 mini cookies), providing roughly 142 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Mini Oreo Cookie — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F11080040 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status 'reviewed'. All per_100g values are taken from this source. Calcium and iron are reported as 0.0 by HPB; see notes for justification.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Chocolate Sandwich Cookies — University of Sydney GI Database (Oreo-type cookies, analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Mini Oreo cookies in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry for chocolate sandwich cookies / cream-filled chocolate biscuits in the University of Sydney GI Database. Per standard GI classification, GI 55 falls in the low category (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70). Glycemic load per 29 g serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (72.69 g × 0.29 = 21.08 g) / 100 = 11.6. The input record cited a GL of 40.0 per 100 g reference portion; the per-serving GL of 11.6 is used here as the clinically relevant figure for a standard 29 g mini pack.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (536 mg/100 g) combined with high sugar (38 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Mini Oreo cookies due to their high sodium content (536 mg/100 g), which approaches the 600 mg/100 g hard threshold, and their high sugar and saturated fat load that further elevates cardiovascular risk; even small portions contribute meaningfully to daily sodium limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (38 g/100 g) and high refined carbohydrate (72.7 g/100 g) with low fibre (2.8 g/100 g), yielding a high glycemic load despite a borderline-low GI of 55
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid Mini Oreo cookies entirely, as the combination of 38 g sugar per 100 g, 72.7 g refined carbohydrate, and only 2.8 g dietary fibre produces a very high glycemic load that causes rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~9.5 g/100 g from palm oil), trans fat (~0.5 g/100 g), high sugar (38 g/100 g), and high sodium (536 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid Mini Oreo cookies because their high saturated fat from palm oil, presence of trans fat, elevated sodium, and high sugar collectively raise LDL cholesterol, promote arterial inflammation, and increase blood pressure, all of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mini Oreo cookies are bite-sized, commercially manufactured chocolate sandwich cookies produced by Nabisco (Mondelez International). Each mini cookie consists of two thin, crisp cocoa-flavoured wafers encasing a sweet cream filling. They are sold in small single-serve packs and are widely available in Singapore supermarkets, convenience stores, and school canteens. The product is ultra-processed, baked, and formulated with enriched wheat flour, sugar, palm oil, cocoa powder, high-fructose corn syrup, leavening agents, salt, and soy lecithin. Multiple flavour variants exist (original, strawberry, blueberry, etc.), all sharing a broadly similar macronutrient profile. Per 100 g, Mini Oreo cookies provide approximately 490 kcal, 4.6 g protein, 20.5 g fat (of which ~9.5 g is saturated fat from palm oil and ~0.5 g is trans fat), 72.7 g carbohydrate, 38 g sugar, 2.8 g dietary fibre, and 536 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low by standard classification: GI ≤55) based on analogous chocolate sandwich cookie data from the University of Sydney GI Database, but the glycemic load per serving remains clinically significant due to the high carbohydrate density. Clinically, Mini Oreo cookies are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (extreme sugar and refined carbohydrate load), hypertension (high sodium and sugar), or cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium). They are a high-calorie, nutrient-poor snack appropriate only for occasional consumption by healthy individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Mini Oreo cookies?
Can diabetics eat Mini Oreo cookies?
Are Mini Oreo cookies high in sugar?
Is Mini Oreo suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F11080040, review_status 'reviewed', confidence 0.95). The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and is clearly documented as such. Saturated fat and trans fat values are estimated from known Oreo product formulation (palm oil base) and are consistent with USDA Branded Food data for similar products.
notes: 1. calcium_mg is reported as 0.0 and iron_mg is reported as 0.0 by the HPB Foodo source (cr_id F11080040). These are confirmed zero values per the HPB database entry for this product; Mini Oreo cookies are not a meaningful source of calcium or iron by formulation. Confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin fields are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, potassium, cholesterol, and micronutrient data for this specific Mini Oreo product are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry and are not available in USDA Branded Food for the exact Singapore SKU as of 2026-04-16. 3. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB Foodo for this product. 4. saturated_fat_g (~9.5 g/100 g) and trans_fat_g (~0.5 g/100 g) are estimated from known palm oil-based Oreo formulation and are consistent with USDA Branded Food entries for comparable Oreo mini cookie products (USDA FDC ID 2103705 and similar); these are flagged as estimated values. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for chocolate sandwich cookies; no direct Mini Oreo GI entry exists in the database. Per standard GI classification (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), a GI of 55 is categorised as low. The input record cited gi_level 'medium' and GL 40.0 per 100 g; gi_category has been corrected to 'low' to match the GI value of 55 per standard classification. Glycemic load per 29 g serving = 55 × 21.08 g available carbohydrate / 100 ≈ 11.6. 6. Halal status: standard Oreo products sold in Singapore may carry MUIS halal certification for specific SKUs; this record does not confirm halal status for all flavour variants and is set to false pending SKU-level verification.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalized input record to RESULT template structure; populated all mandatory sections.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'; verified gi_category alignment.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55 per standard University of Sydney GI classification (low ≤55). Corrected glycemic_load_per_serving from 29.1 to 11.6 (per 29 g serving). Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect corrected GI category. Updated notes section to document the correction.
